author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Slow down the time lapse on the welding and maybe explain it better.  If you use a rivet then you can make an electrical connection that will join the generation between the swivel and the wires.

THAT is a personal contribution to your and Roberts projects; wire into a rivet that can swivel and then all you have to do is combat moisture within the unit.

That makes a 'spark gap' type connection that is free of rotation and keeps you from using a cord that can bind from the rotation from directional winds.

Well done mate! You seem to have that down pat!

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Nice. I have to say the universal motor makes a horrible generator performance wise. In performance these are horrible motors too. The reason why is that these are made as cheap as possible. Less windings, less copper. Giant losses released as heat. Manufacturers get away with that  cause the airflow of a vacuum cleaner passes through the motor. That cools it down and that's how they get away with that. If it's not a vacuum cleaner there's a fan for that reason. If you block a universal motor or any other series motor it's windings will burn in seconds. Try a stepper motor like your dad has covered. Much better  ...

author: @Teknopottu
text: Nice job and creative use of materials but Negative Nathan here asking because no one else did; why did you cut the fin wasting so much material? Was there a reason why it was drawn on the middle of the plate? Not a big deal, just gringing because I hate good materials getting wasted, even accidentally.

replies.author: @tremail564
replies.text: I thought the same time ,why all the little cuts.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thank you mate! The reason I used the middle of the sheet was because it was the flattest part, It was pretty beaten up when I salvaged it. for this reason, I decided to mark it out all in square pieces so some could still be saved. :)

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey Roger that! Now I see, thanks for replying!

author: @SI-GOD
text:
What I want to see is cost effective, stable, AND SAFE ways to make a pole for it that is at least 60 feet in the air. 
I have a 1700 watt wind generator but no pole for it. To buy a pole kit cost more that the gen did. To buy the materials and make my own cost almost as much. 
Any suggestions? 
I wonder if a flag pole with a modified top would be strong enough without guide-wires?
I once saw a couple of street light pole laying in a parking lot (car park) that would have been prefect but could not find the owner to ask about taking them.

replies.author: @185LANDY
replies.text: @totherarf I found a 19mm bolt drilled a hole throw the middle welded it to a plate on one end of the scaffold tube welded the bolt to the plate and bolted a old cam belt tensioner with another plate with a hole in it mounted my (motor)  wind generator then passed my wires throw the bolt I have put have  guide-wires on it works a treat

replies.author: @danp1224
replies.text: I put one up years ago. I used 2 80 diameter pipe that are 3 metres high with a centre pipe that is on a pivot between the other to pipes that is 6 metres long this one has the wind generator on the top of it. Like a seesaw. So the two large pipes go into the ground cemented there welded to each other with heavy flat bar on one side spaced so the 6 meter pipe fits between them both.

author: @SI-GOD
text:
I love the enthusiasm you have. Always keep learning. 
One suggestion... when welding, either zip up that jacket or put on a "poop" shirt so that the sparks don't burn a hole in it. Your mummy will be so mad if you ruin it.  
Yes I went there... had to tease you about so being a baby but it's OK.... some day you will grow up and won't need the bottle any more.  Lol. 
I'm just kidding around.  I hope you take that part as a joke and not be offended.

author: @edwyncorteen1527
text: "all" you need to do now is work out how to get the electricity generated without winding up the take off wires as the mill spins round to face the wind!

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: A piece of string, a loop welded in to allow the string to "wind up" as the wind rotates .... and a weight to pull the thing back to a neutral starting position should the wind stop! Like the old self closing farm gates!

text: Next: Some people use an extension cable that they can unplug and untwist, while others use slip rings to prevent twisting the power chord.

replies.author: @MrAnderson4509
replies.text: First one I ever built I copied the slip ring assembly from a Winco Windcharger, they used to make them in Iowa and a lot of other companies private label them, I guess I should've said that was the first one I built of my design the first wind generator I built was a 1936 Winco and that was the restoration from laying face down in the dirt in Kansas somewhere, the propellers were an unusual design and so are the governors

author: @davidl.howser9707
text: Luke. Way to go. Keep going. Keep exploring. Keep inventing. I watch the channel very often. Selfishly  I LOVE receiving the gift of knowledge offered and received. I love all the details. A suggestion I hope acted on would be to add a camera over the workbench looking down from the ceiling so we could be more successful by split screen and learn more about what is being taught accurately seeing all techniques being used more accurately. Thank you ALL for all the efforts giving knowledge for free and to all.A great way to improve society. Great example for all to follow.  Humanity is in good hands when held by you all there and on display to all to watch. Thank you.
